title: Event-Driven Architecture for SAP
description: Moving from point-to-point RFC to event-driven enterprise. SAP Event Mesh architecture, Enterprise Events in S/4HANA, Kafka integration, Change Data Capture patterns, saga pattern for distributed transactions, idempotency, and event versioning.

sections.title: SAP Event Mesh Architecture

description.value:
SAP Event Mesh is a managed message broker service on SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) that enables event-driven communication between SAP and non-SAP systems. It is built on top of CloudAMQP (RabbitMQ) and Apache Kafka, combined into a unified management plane. It supports both AMQP 1.0 (for enterprise messaging) and MQTT 3.1.1 (for IoT and lightweight clients), with HTTP webhook subscriptions as a third transport option.

Topics and Partitions: An Event Mesh topic is a named channel to which producers publish events and from which consumers subscribe. Topics are hierarchical (e.g., sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed) and support wildcard subscriptions. Under the hood, topics are mapped to Kafka partitions when using the Kafka-compatible REST API, or to RabbitMQ exchanges and queues when using AMQP. The partition count determines the maximum parallelism for consuming events — a topic with N partitions can be consumed by up to N consumer instances concurrently, each reading from a dedicated partition. The Staff+ architect must design topic partitioning to match consumption throughput requirements; too few partitions limit scalability, too many increase ZooKeeper overhead.

Message Broker Internals: Event Mesh provides at-least-once delivery guarantees by default, with optional exactly-once semantics for the Kafka API variant. Messages are persisted to disk based on configurable retention policies (time-based: e.g., 7 days; size-based: e.g., 1 GB). The broker uses a leader-follower replication model for high availability — each partition has a leader (handles all reads/writes) and one or more in-sync replicas (ISRs) that lag behind. If the leader fails, an ISR is elected as the new leader. This is identical to how Kafka achieves fault tolerance, and the same design patterns apply when reasoning about SAP Event Mesh failure scenarios.

SAP Context: For SAP landscapes, Event Mesh replaces traditional point-to-point RFC connections and IDoc-based ALE distribution. Instead of configuring RFC destinations between every pair of systems, you deploy an Event Mesh instance, define topics, and have each system publish and subscribe as needed. This reduces the N×(N-1) connection problem to a hub-and-spoke model with N connections to the mesh, significantly reducing operational overhead at enterprise scale.

sections.codeBlock:
" Event Mesh client setup in ABAP (using Kafka-compatible API)
DATA lo_destination TYPE REF TO if_destination.
DATA lo_producer TYPE REF TO if_edp_producer.

" Get destination configuration (BTP Destination Service)
lo_destination = cl_destination_service=>create(
  iv_destination_name = 'EVENT_MESH' ).

" Create producer for topic
lo_producer = cl_edp_producer_factory=>create(
  iv_topic_arn = 'sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed'
  io_destination = lo_destination ).

" Publish CloudEvent
  lo_producer->publish(
  iv_source        = '/sap/s4/salesorder'
  iv_type          = 'sap.s4.beh.salesorder.v2.Changed'
  iv_id            = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c32( )
  iv_time          = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_time( )
  iv_data          = lv_json_payload
  iv_data_content_type = 'application/json' ).

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Event Mesh vs Apache Kafka — Enterprise Cost Comparison

variables.name: Message throughput
variables.source: Enterprise event volume (PO, SO, invoice changes)

variables.name: Consumer count
variables.source: Data lake, analytics, warehouse, 2 partner systems

event_mesh.monthlyPenalty: $1,200/month — SAP Event Mesh: included in BTP Enterprise License for up to 100K msgs/day. 50ms avg latency. Native SAP integration — no middleware. AMQP 1.0 protocol. Max throughput: ~10K msgs/sec.

kafka_managed.monthlyPenalty: $3,500/month — Confluent Cloud Basic: $1,500 for 50K msgs/day + $2,000 for SAP Kafka Connect connector licensing. 20ms avg latency. Unlimited retention. Requires dedicated operations team for SAP connector maintenance.

calculation.breakeven: Event Mesh is 3x cheaper for standard SAP-native event volumes (<100K msgs/day). Kafka becomes cost-effective at >500K msgs/day or when non-SAP consumers dominate. For pure SAP landscapes, Event Mesh is the recommended choice.

sections.title: Enterprise Events in S/4HANA

description.value:
Enterprise Events in S/4HANA are a framework for publishing business object state changes as events to the SAP Event Mesh. Unlike traditional ABAP techniques (change documents, IDocs, BAPI return structures), Enterprise Events provide a standardized, scalable, and asynchronous mechanism for communicating state changes across the enterprise landscape.

The core configuration is done through transaction C_EVENT_CHANNEL or the /IWXBE/ framework. Here you define: (1) the Event Mesh connection details (URL, authentication, topic namespace); (2) which business objects to observe (e.g., SalesOrder, PurchaseOrder, MaterialMaster); (3) which event types to publish (Created, Changed, Deleted); and (4) the event payload format (default is JSON based on CDS view structure). The system then generates events automatically based on change pointer technology — when a monitored business object is created or modified, the change is captured asynchronously via the update task and published to Event Mesh.

Event Consumption in ABAP: To consume events from Event Mesh in ABAP, you use the CL_EVENT_MESH_RECEIVER class or the higher-level IF_EDP_CONSUMER interface. You create a subscription, define the processing logic, and the framework handles connection management, reconnection, and message acknowledgment. The consumer can run as a background job or as an always-on service using ABAP's STARTING NEW TASK pattern with channel-based subscriptions.

Staff+ Design: At enterprise scale, event consumption must handle: (1) backpressure — if the consumer is slower than the producer, implement throttling with exponential backoff; (2) poison messages — events that cannot be processed should be moved to a dead-letter queue after N retries; (3) ordering guarantees — within a partition, events are ordered, so design your consumer to process events within a partition sequentially; (4) consumer lag monitoring — track the offset lag and alert when it exceeds a threshold (e.g., 10,000 messages or 5 minutes).

" Consuming Enterprise Events in ABAP

CLASS /dmo/cl_event_consumer DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_edp_consumer_handler.
    METHODS start_consuming.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS /dmo/cl_event_consumer IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD start_consuming.
    DATA: lo_consumer TYPE REF TO if_edp_consumer.

    " Create consumer for SalesOrder.Changed topic
    lo_consumer = cl_edp_consumer_factory=>create(
      iv_topic_arn     = 'sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed'
      iv_subscription  = 'SalesOrderChangeHandler'
      iv_handler_class = me
    ).

    " Start consuming (runs in background)
    lo_consumer->start( ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_edp_consumer_handler~on_event.
    " Deserialize event payload
    DATA: ls_event TYPE /dmo/salesorder_event.
    
    /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
      EXPORTING json = iv_payload
      CHANGING  data = ls_event
    ).
    
    CASE ls_event-event_type.
      WHEN 'CHANGED'.
        " Process the change event
        PROCESS_SALESORDER_CHANGE( ls_event-new_data ).
        
        " Acknowledge (commit offset)
        lo_message->ack( ).
        
      WHEN 'DELETED'.
        " Handle deletion
        ARCHIVE_SALESORDER( ls_event-old_data ).
        lo_message->ack( ).
        
      WHEN OTHERS.
        " Unknown event type — move to DLQ
        lo_message->nack( ).
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" Enterprise Event consumer in ABAP
CLASS /dmo/cl_event_consumer DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_edp_consumer_handler.
    METHODS start_consuming.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS /dmo/cl_event_consumer IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD start_consuming.
    DATA: lo_consumer TYPE REF TO if_edp_consumer.
    lo_consumer = cl_edp_consumer_factory=>create(
      iv_topic_arn     = 'sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed'
      iv_subscription  = 'SalesOrderChangeHandler'
      iv_handler_class = me ).
    lo_consumer->start( ).
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD if_edp_consumer_handler~on_event.
    DATA: ls_event TYPE /dmo/salesorder_event.
    /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
      EXPORTING json = iv_payload
      CHANGING  data = ls_event ).
    CASE ls_event-event_type.
      WHEN 'CHANGED'.
        process_salesorder_change( ls_event-new_data ).
        lo_message->ack( ).
      WHEN 'DELETED'.
        archive_salesorder( ls_event-old_data ).
        lo_message->ack( ).
      WHEN OTHERS.
        lo_message->nack( ).
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

sections.title: Kafka for SAP Integration

description.value:
Apache Kafka has become the de-facto standard for event streaming in the tech industry, used by FAANG companies and enterprises alike. For SAP landscapes, Kafka serves as the central nervous system that connects SAP systems to the broader enterprise technology stack. Understanding why FAANG chose Kafka over traditional message brokers (IBM MQ, ActiveMQ, RabbitMQ) is essential for Staff+ architectural reasoning.

Why FAANG Uses Kafka: Kafka was designed at LinkedIn to handle three specific requirements that traditional brokers could not meet: (1) high throughput — Kafka achieves millions of messages per second through sequential disk I/O, zero-copy transfer via sendfile(), and batching; (2) durability and replay — Kafka persists messages to disk with configurable retention, allowing consumers to replay from any point in time; (3) distributed architecture — Kafka partitions topics across multiple brokers for horizontal scalability and fault tolerance. These same requirements apply to SAP enterprise integration: an SAP-to-Kafka bridge must handle peak loads of 50,000+ IDoc messages per hour, provide exactly-once semantics for financial transactions, and allow replay for failure recovery.

How SAP Fits: SAP integrates with Kafka through multiple pathways. The SAP Event Mesh provides a Kafka-compatible REST API, allowing Kafka producers and consumers to publish and subscribe to Event Mesh topics as if they were native Kafka topics. For high-throughput scenarios, the SAP Kafka Connect Connector (available as part of SAP Integration Suite) provides a managed connector for streaming data between S/4HANA and Confluent Kafka or Amazon MSK. For on-premise SAP landscapes, the ABAP Kafka Client Library (CL_KAFKA_PRODUCER, CL_KAFKA_CONSUMER in ABAP 7.55+) allows direct Kafka integration from ABAP without intermediate middleware.

Architecture Patterns: The three most common SAP-Kafka integration patterns are: (1) CDC to Kafka — use SAP's change data capture to stream table changes to Kafka for downstream processing; (2) Event Mesh to Kafka Bridge — Event Mesh subscribes to S/4HANA events and forwards them to a corporate Kafka cluster; (3) ABAP Native Kafka — ABAP application code produces and consumes Kafka messages directly, useful for real-time analytics feeds and operational data synchronization. The Staff+ engineer must evaluate throughput, latency, and operational complexity tradeoffs for each pattern.

sections.codeBlock:
" ABAP Kafka producer — direct integration
DATA lo_kafka_producer TYPE REF TO cl_kafka_producer.
DATA lv_message TYPE string.

try.
    lo_kafka_producer = cl_kafka_producer=>create(
      iv_bootstrap_servers = 'kafka-cluster:9092'
      iv_acks = 1  " 1 = leader ack, -1 = all replicas
      iv_compression_type = 'snappy' ).

    lv_message = |{ '{"SalesOrder":"1001","Action":"Created"}' }|.

    lo_kafka_producer->send_sync(
      iv_topic   = 'sap.sd.salesorder'
      iv_key     = '1001'
      iv_value   = lv_message
      iv_partition = 0 ).

    " Verify delivery
    DATA(lv_offset) = lo_kafka_producer->get_last_offset( ).
  catch cx_kafka_error INTO DATA(lx_kafka).
    " Handle production error with retry
endtry.

sections.title: Change Data Capture

description.value:
Change Data Capture (CDC) is the process of identifying, capturing, and delivering changes made to a database as a stream of events. In SAP landscapes, CDC is the most reliable mechanism for keeping downstream systems synchronized with SAP data. Unlike polling-based approaches (querying every N seconds for changed records), CDC captures changes at the database transaction log level, providing exactly-once semantics with minimal latency.

SAP CDC Approaches: There are three approaches to CDC in SAP landscapes. (1) Application-Level CDC uses SAP's change document framework (transaction SCDO) to capture business object changes. This is the most semantically rich approach — changes include before/after images, change reason, and user context. However, it only captures changes through SAP business objects, not direct database modifications. (2) Trigger-Based CDC uses database triggers on HANA tables to capture changes to a separate change log table. This captures all changes (including direct SQL modifications) but adds overhead to every write operation. (3) Log-Based CDC reads the HANA redo log to extract changes without any modification to the application or database schema. Tools like SAP DataSphere, SLT (SAP Landscape Transformation), and third-party CDC platforms (Debezium, Qlik) use this approach for zero-impact capture.

Downstream Consumption: Once captured, CDC events are typically published to Kafka or Event Mesh for consumption by downstream systems. The event payload contains: the operation type (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), the key of the changed record, the before-image (optional for update/delete), the after-image (for insert/update), and metadata (timestamp, transaction ID). The downstream consumer applies these changes to its own data store to maintain synchronization.

Staff+ Design: CDC at enterprise scale requires addressing: (1) Schema evolution — when the source table schema changes, the CDC stream must handle field additions, deletions, and type changes without breaking consumers; (2) Backfill — when a new consumer joins, it needs the entire history of changes, requiring a batch backfill before streaming; (3) Latency vs Throughput — log-based CDC introduces minimal latency (sub-second), but trigger-based CDC can impact transactional throughput by 10-20%; (4) Consistency guarantees — changes to a single business object that spans multiple tables must be captured as a consistent, ordered group of events.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 380" sections.codeBlock:
" CDC via HANA change detection & publication
" Captures material master changes to Kafka
CLASS zcl_cdc_material IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD capture_changes.
    " Read from CDC log table populated by DB trigger
    SELECT * FROM zcdc_material_log
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_changes)
      WHERE processed = @abap_false
      ORDER BY change_id UP TO 100 ROWS.
    LOOP AT lt_changes INTO DATA(ls_change).
      DATA(lv_event) = /ui2/cl_json=>serialize(
        data = VALUE #(
          operation = ls_change-operation  " I/U/D
          key = ls_change-matnr
          before = ls_change-old_data
          after = ls_change-new_data
          changed_at = ls_change-changed_at
          tid = ls_change-luw_id ) ).
      publish_to_event_mesh(
        iv_topic = 'sap/mm/material/Changed'
        iv_data  = lv_event ).
      UPDATE zcdc_material_log
        SET processed = @abap_true
        WHERE change_id = @ls_change-change_id.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

description.value:
Event-driven architectures require well-defined patterns for handling asynchronous processing workflows. Three patterns are essential for enterprise SAP integration: the Outbox Pattern, the Saga Pattern, and the Process Orchestrator.

Outbox Pattern: The outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem — when a single business operation must write to both a database and a message queue, atomicity is at risk. The solution: write the event to an outbox table in the same database transaction as the business data. A separate process (the outbox publisher) reads from the outbox table and publishes events to the message broker. This ensures exactly-once delivery: if the publisher crashes before publishing, it resumes from the last committed outbox record on restart. In ABAP, the outbox pattern is implemented using SAP's update task (V1/V2) or by writing to a custom outbox table with a separate background job as publisher.

Saga Pattern: The saga pattern manages distributed transactions across multiple services where a single ACID transaction is not feasible. A saga is a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating transaction for rollback. For example, in a cross-SAP-system order-to-cash process: CreateOrder (S/4HANA) → ReserveInventory (EWM) → ProcessPayment (CFIN) → ShipGoods (TM). If ProcessPayment fails, the saga executes compensating transactions: CancelOrder, ReleaseInventory. There are two saga implementations: choreography-based (each service publishes events and listens for compensating events) and orchestration-based (a central coordinator tells each service what to do). For SAP landscapes, orchestration-based sagas via SAP Integration Suite or Workflow Management are preferred because they provide visibility and manageability.

Process Orchestrator: SAP's Workflow Management or BPM-based orchestration manages long-running processes with human approval steps, timeouts, and exception handling. The orchestrator maintains process state, routes events to the appropriate handler, and manages compensation. At Staff+ level, you must understand when to use a workflow engine versus event-driven choreography — orchestration is better for processes with human decisions and complex branching; choreography is better for high-throughput, automated pipelines with simple decision logic.

Create Order</text><path d="M200,158 L310,158" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="saga-ok" x="310" y="140" width="100" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="360" y="163">2. Reserve Inv</text><path d="M410,158 L520,158" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="saga-ok" x="520" y="140" width="100" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="570" y="163">3. Payment</text><path d="M570,176 L570,220" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="saga-comp" x="520" y="220" width="100" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="570" y="243">Comp: Refund</text><path d="M410,176 L410,220" class="saga-comp" stroke="#e74c3c" stroke-dasharray="4"/><rect class="saga-comp" x="310" y="220" width="100" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="360" y="243">Comp: ReleaseInv</text><path d="M200,176 L200,220" class="saga-comp" stroke="#e74c3c" stroke-dasharray="4"/><rect class="saga-comp" x="100" y="220" width="100" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="150" y="243">Comp: Cancel</text><path d="M150,300 L150,340" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="60" y="340" width="180" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="150" y="363">Choreography: Event-driven</text><rect class="sys-box" x="460" y="340" width="180" height="36"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="550" y="363">Orchestration: Coordinator</text><text class="lbl" x="350" y="300">Failure at step 3 triggers compensation</text><path d="M360,176 L360,220" class="saga-comp" stroke="#e74c3c" stroke-dasharray="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-red)"/></svg>

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use the outbox pattern for publishing events from this ABAP transaction?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Can the database write and the event publication happen in the same transaction?" If yes (both write to HANA), you can use direct publication. If the event goes to an external broker (Event Mesh, Kafka), you need the outbox pattern for atomicity.

branches.condition: Database write + external broker publish (dual-write problem)
branches.path: Outbox pattern. Write event to outbox table in same LUW as business data. Separate publisher process reads outbox and publishes to broker. Ensures exactly-once delivery. Use for all external event publications.

branches.condition: Both business data and event stay in the same HANA database
branches.path: Direct event publication. Use ABAP raise event or UPDATE task (V1/V2). Both happen in the same database LUW. No dual-write problem. Simpler — no outbox table or publisher process needed.
Outbox with batch publisher. Buffer events in outbox table up to 500 or 5 seconds. Publish in batch to Event Mesh. Reduces broker connections. Add dead-letter queue for failed publications.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem by making event publication part of the business transaction. The event is guaranteed to be published if and only if the business data is committed.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Outbox Pattern — Atomicity Guarantee vs Latency Cost

direct_publish.monthlyPenalty: $0 — Direct: Write business data + publish event in same ABAP LUW. 30ms total. Zero latency. Risk: if broker is down, the entire transaction fails.

outbox_async.monthlyPenalty: $0 — Outbox: Business write (5ms) + async publisher (avg 2.5s delay due to 5s interval). Maximum 5s delay. Broker failure doesn't affect business transaction. Adds ~2.5s latency but eliminates coupling.

calculation.breakeven: Outbox adds ~2.5s latency but eliminates transaction failures due to broker unavailability. At event volumes <1M/day, the outbox latency is imperceptible to users. Use outbox for all external event publication where business transaction reliability is critical.

sections.title: Idempotency & Exactly-Once

description.value:
Idempotency is the property that an operation can be applied multiple times without changing the result beyond the first application. In event-driven systems, idempotency is the foundation for reliable message processing. Without it, a network failure that causes a message to be redelivered could result in duplicate sales orders, double payments, or inconsistent inventory counts.

Exactly-Once vs At-Least-Once: Message brokers provide different delivery guarantees. At-most-once means the message may be lost but never duplicated — safe for non-critical data like analytics. At-least-once means the message is guaranteed to be delivered, but it may be delivered multiple times — this is the default for SAP Event Mesh and Kafka. Exactly-once means the message is delivered exactly once — this is the hardest to achieve and typically requires idempotent consumers combined with transactional producers. In SAP contexts, exactly-once semantics are critical for financial transactions and inventory movements where duplicates have real financial impact.

Idempotency Key Pattern: The standard pattern for achieving idempotency is the idempotency key. The producer generates a unique key (typically a UUID) for each logical operation and includes it in the event payload. The consumer maintains a deduplication store (a database table or Redis set) of processed keys. When processing an event, the consumer first checks if the key has been processed. If yes, it acknowledges the event without processing (idempotent replay). If no, it processes the event and records the key atomically with the processing result. This ensures that even if the same event is delivered twice, the business effect is the same as if it was delivered once.

SAP Implementation: In ABAP, the idempotency key pattern is implemented using a dedup table with a unique index on the idempotency key. The processing logic uses INSERT ... FROM ... with exception handling for duplicate key violations. For high-throughput scenarios, consider using HANA's native dedup capabilities or a Redis-based dedup store. The dedup record must be cleaned up after a retention period (e.g., 7 days) to prevent unbounded table growth — implement as a scheduled cleanup job.

" Idempotency Key Pattern in ABAP

CLASS /dmo/cl_idempotent_processor DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS process_event
      IMPORTING iv_idempotency_key TYPE string
                iv_payload        TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(rv_result)  TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS /dmo/cl_idempotent_processor IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD process_event.
    " Check if already processed using INSERT ... EXCEPTIONS
    " Unique index on idempotency_key prevents duplicates
    INSERT INTO zd_idempotency_log FROM VALUE #(
      idempotency_key = iv_idempotency_key
      created_at      = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_time( )
      status          = 'PROCESSING'
    ).
    
    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      " First time — process the event
      TRY.
          rv_result = process_business_logic( iv_payload ).
          
          " Mark as completed
          UPDATE zd_idempotency_log
            SET status = 'COMPLETED'
            WHERE idempotency_key = iv_idempotency_key.
          
        CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_error).
          " Mark as failed (can be retried)
          UPDATE zd_idempotency_log
            SET status = 'FAILED',
                error_message = lx_error->get_text( )
            WHERE idempotency_key = iv_idempotency_key.
          RAISE EXCEPTION lx_error.
      ENDTRY.
      
    ELSEIF sy-subrc = 4.
      " Duplicate key — already processed
      " Read the previous result (idempotent replay)
      SELECT SINGLE status, result
        FROM zd_idempotency_log
        INTO @DATA(ls_existing)
        WHERE idempotency_key = @iv_idempotency_key.
      
      IF sy-subrc = 0 AND ls_existing-status = 'COMPLETED'.
        rv_result = ls_existing-result.
        RETURN.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
  
  " Cleanup job — run daily to purge old keys
  METHOD cleanup_idempotency_log.
    DELETE FROM zd_idempotency_log
      WHERE created_at < cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_time( )
        - cl_abap_timestamps=>days_to_ts( 7 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" Outbox pattern — atomic save + event publication
METHOD create_salesorder_with_outbox.
  " 1. Write business data + outbox in same LUW
  MODIFY ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_salesorder
    ENTITY salesorder
    CREATE FROM ls_order
    FAILED DATA(ls_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(ls_reported).
  IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL. RETURN. ENDIF.
  " 2. Insert outbox record (same DB commit)
  INSERT INTO zoutbox VALUES @(
    event_id    = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c32( )
    event_type  = 'SalesOrder.Created'
    payload     = lv_json
    status      = 'P'  " Pending
    created_at  = utclong_current( ) ).
  COMMIT WORK. " Single commit for both
  " 3. Background job picks up outbox records
  "    and publishes to Event Mesh asynchronously
ENDMETHOD.

" Saga: compensating action on failure
METHOD compensate_create_salesorder.
  IF iv_action = 'CREATE' AND iv_status = 'FAILED'.
    " Reverse the order
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_salesorder
      ENTITY salesorder
      DELETE FROM VALUE #(
        ( salesorder_id = iv_order_id ) ).
  ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our event processing creates duplicate sales orders when the Event Mesh redelivers messages. The 'at-least-once' guarantee is causing data corruption."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Switch the queue to exactly-once delivery." (may not be supported by all brokers and adds latency)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "At-least-once delivery means duplicates are inevitable — the solution is not to prevent redelivery but to make processing idempotent. Here's the implementation: (1) Add an idempotency key to every event — typically a UUID generated by the producer. The event type + key must be unique per logical operation. (2) In the consumer, create a dedup table with a unique index on (event_type, idempotency_key). Use INSERT ... EXCEPTIONS — if sy-subrc = 0, this is the first delivery; process and update status to 'COMPLETED'. If sy-subrc = 4 (duplicate key), skip processing and return the cached result. (3) The dedup table needs a cleanup job — delete records older than 7 days (matching Event Mesh retention). (4) For critical financial events, don't just dedup — implement exactly-once processing by storing the processing result in the same database transaction as the dedup record. If the process crashes between processing and marking complete, the next redelivery will see the 'PROCESSING' status and the event handler should check whether the processing actually completed before retrying. This pattern is called 'transactional outbox with idempotent processing' — it's the standard FAANG approach for exactly-once semantics at scale."
adversarialResponse.pattern: idempotency-first — explain why at-least-once is the right delivery semantic, provide a complete implementation (dedup table, unique constraint, status tracking), and connect to the FAANG-standard transactional outbox pattern.

sections.title: Event Versioning Strategy

description.value:
Event versioning is the practice of evolving event schemas over time without breaking existing consumers. In an enterprise SAP landscape, events are consumed by multiple downstream systems (analytics platforms, data lakes, microservices, partner systems) — and you do not control all of them. A breaking schema change that goes unversioned can cause cascading failures across the enterprise.

Schema Evolution Approaches: There are three main approaches: (1) Schema Registry with Avro/Protobuf — use Confluent Schema Registry (or SAP's equivalent) with Avro or Protobuf schemas. The registry enforces compatibility rules (backward, forward, full). This is the FAANG-standard approach used at LinkedIn, Uber, and Netflix. (2) JSON Schema Versioning — embed a version field in the JSON event payload and maintain version-specific consumers. Simpler than Avro but requires manual compatibility validation. (3) Adding without Breaking — the simplest approach: only add optional fields (never remove or rename) and use semantic versioning on the event type. This works for small-scale landscapes but becomes unmanageable beyond 20+ consumers.

Compatibility Rules: Backward compatible means new consumers can read old events — achieved by having default values for new fields. Forward compatible means old consumers can read new events — achieved by ignoring unknown fields. Full compatible means both directions work — this requires both default values and unknown field tolerance. For SAP events, backward compatibility is mandatory (old consumers must still work), and forward compatibility is strongly recommended (allows rolling upgrades of consumers).

SAP Event Versioning: SAP's Enterprise Events use a URL-based versioning scheme: sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed. The version (v2) is part of the topic name. When a breaking change is needed, a new topic is created (e.g., v3) and both versions run simultaneously until all consumers have migrated. SAP recommends a minimum migration window of 6 months for major version changes. The Staff+ architect must design a topic naming convention that supports versioning, plan the migration timeline, and ensure that consumers are tested against the new schema before cutover.

sections.codeBlock:
" Event versioning with CloudEvents type string
" Each version is a separate topic
" v1: sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v1/Changed
" v2: sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed

" Producer: publish with versioned type
DATA ls_event TYPE /dmo/cl_ce_entity=>ty_cloud_event.
ls_event = VALUE #(
  specversion = '1.0'
  id          = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c32( )
  source      = '/sap/s4/salesorder'
  type        = 'sap.s4.beh.salesorder.v2.Changed'
  time        = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_time( )
  data        = lv_json_payload ).

" Consumer: subscribe to specific version
" Register handler for v2
lo_consumer = cl_edp_consumer_factory=>create(
  iv_topic_arn    = 'sap/s4/beh/salesorder/v2/Changed'
  iv_subscription = 'SalesOrderHandlerV2'
  iv_handler_class = me ).

" When migrating: run both consumers in parallel
" Route 10% traffic to v2, monitor, then switch

details: Staff+ Insight: Event-driven architecture is a core FAANG concept (Kafka, Pulsar, EventBridge). The ability to design event-driven enterprise systems demonstrates distributed systems thinking. Frame SAP Event Mesh discussions as "enterprise event bus" — comparing to Kafka, AWS EventBridge, and Azure Event Grid.
